name: Negation (안 and 못)
summary: Say what you don't do with 안 and what you can't do with 못, the two main negation words.
explanation:
Korean has two short negation words placed right before the verb. 안 (an) means simple 'not' and expresses choice or fact: 안 가요 (an gayo, I don't go). 못 (mot) means 'cannot' and expresses inability or an outside obstacle: 못 가요 (mot gayo, I can't go).

For 하다 verbs, the negation slips between the noun part and 하다. 공부 안 해요 (gongbu an haeyo, I don't study) and 공부 못 해요 (gongbu mot haeyo, I can't study) are both correct; 안 공부해요 sounds wrong.

There is also a longer negative form using -지 않다 for 안 and -지 못하다 for 못: 가지 않아요 (gaji anayo, I don't go), 가지 못해요 (gaji mothaeyo, I can't go). It means the same thing but feels slightly more formal or written. Start with the short 안 and 못 for everyday speech.
pattern: 안/못 + verb  |  verb stem + 지 않다 / 지 못하다

examples.native: 저는 고기를 안 먹어요. (jeoneun gogireul an meogeoyo.)
오늘은 일을 안 해요. (oneureun ireul an haeyo.)
시간이 없어서 못 갔어요. (sigani eopseoseo mot gasseoyo.)
examples.en: I couldn't go because I had no time.

examples.native: 그 영화는 재미없지 않아요. (geu yeonghwaneun jaemieopji anayo.)

examples.en: It's too spicy, so I can't eat it.

commonMistakes: Mixing up 안 and 못. 안 means you choose not to; 못 means you are unable to.
Placing 안 before the noun in a 하다 verb. Say 공부 안 해요, not 안 공부해요.
Putting 안 or 못 after the verb. They always come directly before the verb.
Forgetting that 못 needs a real obstacle or inability; for a plain 'not', use 안.
Doubling up negation by using both 안 and -지 않다 together in one verb.
tip: 안 = won't or don't (choice). 못 = can't (obstacle). Both sit right before the verb.
